planting.note: Plant container vines in late winter or spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; provide a sturdy trellis, pergola, or arbor that can support the eventual weight of a mature vine — wisteria vines become very heavy over decades.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade on a sturdy support structure; Amethyst Falls is the most popular and most widely planted cultivar of Wisteria frutescens — the American native wisteria — offering all the beauty of classic wisteria with critically better behavior in the garden. Asian wisterias (W. sinensis and W. floribunda) are considered invasive in many Eastern and Gulf South states and can escape cultivation into natural areas; American wisteria (W. frutescens) is a well-behaved native that produces similar, lightly fragrant, lavender-to-violet flower racemes without the invasive spreading of its Asian cousins. Amethyst Falls was selected for early bloom age (typically flowering in its second or third year, compared to 5–10 years for species wisteria), repeat blooming through summer after the main spring flush, and shorter flower racemes (4–6 inches) that are proportionally appropriate for smaller garden structures. The flowers are fragrant but less intensely so than Asian wisterias. In zone 9, Amethyst Falls blooms reliably from April through June with lighter repeat bloom flushes through summer. The vigorous twining habit quickly covers an arbor, pergola, or fence and the compound, dark-green deciduous foliage provides summer shade after bloom. Prune after bloom to control size; established plants tolerate hard pruning.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; space at least 18 inches apart — good air circulation is essential to minimize powdery mildew, the primary phlox disease in humid climates.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade with good air circulation; David was named the Perennial Plant of the Year in 2002 and is widely considered the best white tall garden phlox cultivar ever introduced — selected by David Benner of New Hope, Pennsylvania, specifically for outstanding resistance to powdery mildew, the devastating fungal disease that disfigures most Phlox paniculata cultivars by midsummer in humid climates. Most phlox cultivars develop severe powdery mildew by July or August in the East and Gulf South — their leaves turn white with fungal coating that persists through the rest of the season. David's powdery mildew resistance is dramatically superior to older cultivars, maintaining clean, attractive dark-green foliage well into fall. The large, fragrant, pure white flower heads (panicles) are produced from July through September in zone 8, making it one of the finest long-season white perennials. The intensely fragrant flowers are outstanding for cutting and garden performance. In zone 9, tall garden phlox is not reliably perennial — it performs best as a short-lived perennial in zone 8 and colder. In zone 9's intense summer heat, phlox often declines, but with afternoon shade and consistent moisture it can be maintained for 2–3 years.
sources: Perennial Plant Association — Perennial Plant of the Year 2002

common_name: Bright Eyes Phlox (Pink-with-Red-Eye Tall Garden Phlox)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; plant in groupings of 3–5 plants for the best garden effect — a single Bright Eyes plant is less impressive than a cluster, which creates the large mass of color characteristic of traditional phlox borders.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Bright Eyes is a classic tall garden phlox cultivar producing large, rounded flower heads in soft pink with a distinctive dark crimson-red eye at the center of each five-petaled flower — the bicolor pink-and-red pattern creating a vibrant, cheerful flower that has been a garden favorite since the early twentieth century. The large, domed panicles (8–12 inches across) are sweetly fragrant and produced on upright stems 30–42 inches tall in July through September. The contrasting eye pattern gives each individual flower an appealing depth and complexity that pure-color phlox cultivars lack. The fragrance is one of the most pleasant in the summer garden. As with all Phlox paniculata cultivars, powdery mildew can be a problem in humid climates — Bright Eyes is somewhat susceptible, so provide excellent air circulation (wide spacing, no overhead watering) and treat preventively with sulfur or neem oil sprays if mildew is a recurring problem. Deadheading the central panicle when it fades promotes side-shoot flowering and extends the season. In zone 9, tall phlox is treated as a short-lived perennial — it grows most reliably in zone 4–8 and may decline after 1–2 seasons in the intense summer heat of zone 9.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rhizomes in spring after soil warms in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Spiral Ginger is one of the most cold-tolerant Costus species — surviving to zone 9 (and marginally zone 8) with root protection during freezes.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Spiral Ginger is a tropical shade perennial in the Costus genus — producing erect, cane-like stems with the characteristic spiral arrangement of broad, tropical-looking leaves that give the entire Costus group their common name. The leaves are arranged in a beautiful, precise spiral up each cane that is architecturally distinctive and visually arresting. In late summer and fall, large (3–4 inch), white, tissue-paper-thin flowers emerge from reddish-brown cone-like terminal bracts, providing a dramatic contrast against the deep-green foliage. Spiral Ginger creates outstanding tropical shade garden effects in zone 9–11, where its combination of bold, spiraling foliage and dramatic late-season flowers provides multi-season ornamental interest. In zone 9, the tops are killed by hard freezes but the underground rhizomes typically survive to zone 9a when mulched, re-sprouting vigorously in spring. The bold tropical foliage texture combines beautifully with other shade tropical plants — caladiums, gingers, and philodendrons — to create a lush, jungle-like understory effect under high-canopy trees. Division of established clumps every 3–4 years maintains vigor and provides divisions for expanding the planting.

common_name: Di Cicco Broccoli (Italian Sprouting Broccoli, Continuous-Harvest Broccoli)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; in zone 9, transplant in September–October for the main fall crop — the primary production window is October through March, with spring planting often too brief before heat ends production in May.

directions: Set out transplants in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun during cool seasons; Di Cicco is a classic Italian heirloom sprouting broccoli dating to the nineteenth century — producing smaller, blue-green central heads followed by prolific side shoots (sideshoots) that continue producing edible florets for weeks or months after the central head is harvested. Unlike compact heading broccoli (Calabrese, Marathon) that produces one large head and then is done, Di Cicco is a continuous-harvest type — the plant continues to produce side shoot clusters for 6–10 weeks after the main head is cut, giving each plant a much longer productive season. In zone 9, Di Cicco is best planted in September–October for fall production, with continued harvest through the winter months. The open, branching plant habit and the loose, smaller head size of Di Cicco give it a more old-fashioned, informal appearance compared to tight-headed modern cultivars — but the flavor and the extended harvest period make it an excellent choice for the Gulf Coast kitchen garden where a long, productive cool-season broccoli is needed. Cold-tolerant to light freezes; protect from temperatures below 26°F.
